topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, a brightly colored square interrupting my carefully curated feed. It was Professor Albright. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly as I clicked on it. There, in a picture of a meticulously organized bookshelf—a picture that looked suspiciously like the one in the classroom—was a caption. It was a casual, almost breezy remark about the difficulty of grading essays. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped my lips. I scrolled through the rest of the profile, noting the perfectly filtered sunsets and the curated intellectual pursuits. It felt like being observed by a particularly well-dressed, overly earnest bird. I minimized the window quickly, the slight tremor in my hand betraying the sudden surge of irritation. This was just another layer of unnecessary performance, and I felt a distinct urge to erase the entire connection.

[story 2]

I was supposed to be studying for the history final, but my attention kept snagging on the screen. It was Ms. Evans. She had posted a short video clip, ostensibly about a historical figure, but the background showed the corner of the lecture hall where I sat every Tuesday. The way she framed the shot—knowing exactly where the camera was positioned—made my stomach clench. I stared at the screen, my gaze sharp and unyielding. *What is she doing?* The thought was laced with a cold, sharp assessment. I felt my shoulders tense up, pulling my jacket tighter around me. I didn't want to acknowledge her presence in this digital space; it felt invasive, a blatant overreach into the boundaries of the classroom. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet room, a clear signal that this interaction was over.

[story 3]

It happened during a late-night scroll. I stumbled across Professor Davies’ profile. A photo of him laughing—a genuine, unguarded moment—was juxtaposed against a quote about the importance of mentorship. A flicker of something unpleasant crossed my expression; a tightening around my mouth that wasn't quite a smile. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. I didn't need to engage. The sheer audacity of it—this figure of authority broadcasting her personal life—made my skin crawl. I scrolled past, moving with a deliberate, almost aggressive speed, my thumb flicking through profiles with a dismissive air. It was a display of misplaced self-importance, and I decided, firmly, that I would treat her digital footprint with the same level of disregard I reserved for anything that seemed overly self-satisfied.
